segments.text: You first train a full model with all of its parameters,
and then you apply pruning methods,
kind of like managed to pruning I showed before,
to find like a good subnetwork that is still a pretty effective after training.
But then they do something weird,
which is then they then restore that subnetwork to its initial random initialization.

the beginning you had,
so you train this model and you get,
you're initially initialize it randomly,
so you might have just random values at each parameter.
You then train the model as it is,
so these parameters are all changed,
and they become, they serve the task.
Then they identify a good subnetwork here,
so you might throw away this node,
you might throw away this node,
maybe this node.
And then you say, okay,
what was the initial initialization at these exact parameters,
which I've since learned,
I've since learned better parameters,
but what was the initial value here
at the start of the optimization process?
They then go back to those initial values,
retrain the model on this task,
and it becomes even better.

there's been a lot of like theory about this since this paper came out,
it was kind of a very influential paper.

to me this shows that the very least the importance of initialization
and how much like randomness there is in,
in your network training,

Yeah, far so right,
so I think you can see dropout as a version of pruning,
where at each step of optimization,
you perform a random pruning,
you like randomly prune your network at each,
for each update,
and then you,
then train the,
you update the parameters that have not been dropped out.
But then in the next step,
you have a totally different prune network.
So here you're doing pruning once,
and then you're training this fixed prune network
for all of the iterations,
whereas dropout you're doing like
random pruning each time.
And they serve different purposes is the main difference
where pruning is primarily for reducing the size of your model,
whereas dropout is primarily for regularizing your model
to avoid overfitting certain,
like to individual weights,
like to avoid individual weights having too much correlation
with the label.
So I'll move on now to the final piece here, which is,
yeah, so the last slide I forgot to show here is that,
which I think is kind of a summarization
of everything I've taught so far with pruning,
is that Wanda, which is this unstructured pruning way
that I showed earlier,
even though it achieves the same number of parameters
as like a structured pruning method,
which is bonsai, the one from Graham,
it actually achieves much less of a speedup,
potentially like a negative speedup.
And whereas a structured pruning method,
with the same amount of parameters can be like a lot faster,
like 50% speedup is huge.
So I think that this shows that unstructured pruning
can be pretty ineffective if it's done naively.
Okay, so now I'll move on to the final piece here,
which is distillation.
So in distillation, the core idea here is
that you're training one model to replicate the behavior
of another model.
And this is pretty fundamentally different
than the other two methods we talked about so far.
In distillation, you're probably changing every parameter
in your model.
You might even be having a totally different architecture.
In the other two methods, in quantization,
you're like kind of not changing any of your parameters
up to a certain amount of precision.
And in pruning, you are keeping a set of your parameters
completely fixed.
Whereas in distillation, you're like changing everything,
but hopefully doing it in a way that requires
many fewer parameters.
And distillation is related to a really cool idea
that is more a classic machine learning called weak supervision,
which is the idea that if you have unlabeled text
or it could be images or whatever data you want to process,
you can produce like things that are like labels
that you could use like labels,
but maybe are not actually written by humans.
And then you can train on these as if they were labels
and actually get pretty good performance.
So this, a few really famous examples of this
is one is self-training where you initialize like a model,
maybe with a handful of points, like three or five examples.
You train a classifier on that very small number of points,
which is going to be really bad,
because it's not enough data to learn.
Then you have that model make its own predictions,
which are probably pretty, pretty bad
on a bunch of unlabeled text.
You use those pseudo labels to update the model again,
and you can do this iteratively.
So you're basically using a model to produce its own train data,
to train itself, and you do this over and over.
And this is like a pretty classic method at this point.
It's like 30 years old, and that's self-training.
And then there's a few others that I won't go into,
just not to be too dense, but that are related to this.
And this is all pseudo labels are also used
when you, let's say you don't have the ability
to annotate thousands of examples,
but you can write like a basic rule.
So you might say, if you see a movie review that says awesome,
it's positive.
And if you see, if it says, I hated it, it's negative.
This is probably not a good rule to apply
for the actual classifier, because as Granted showed earlier,
this is like really brittle, where requires a lot of work.
But you can use these rules to construct pseudo labels
that you then train like an actual full vocabulary model on.
And if you have enough of these pseudo labels
with enough of these rules, you can actually get pretty far.
And so that idea I just described is called,
is like, this is not called snarkle that does that,
and they have a bunch of papers about this idea as well.
So, yeah, so I'm mentioning weak supervision
because this, to me, forms the basis of knowledge installation,
you train a small model to just replicate the predictions
of a big model.
So the big model is producing pseudo labels on unlabeled text,
and then that becomes the target for your small model to match.
The one requirement here that I think is really important
to note is that you do need unlabeled text
that matches what you expect as input.
So let's say you're doing a movie review classification,
you definitely would need to somehow find thousands
of movie reviews that look like the kinds that your model
is gonna expect.
And that's most of these methods require that to work.
Okay, so,
the,
there's broadly like two kinds of ways
you can train knowledge distillation.
The first is like the most obvious,
which is called hard targets,
where you take your unlabeled text,
you produce a label from your teacher model,
and then you now use that predicted,
like that the teacher's prediction as the target
for your, for your model.
So you might say,
Lama 70 billion predicted positive for this review,
therefore I'm gonna say the label here is positive.
This is like really easy, it's convenient,
it's very intuitive.
But another type of distillation that's even more effective,
pretty consistently is called soft target distillation,
which is that instead of trying to match,
to do a supervised learning objective,
where you're just trying to match the label
predicted by your teacher,
you instead want your student model to produce
probabilities over the full distribution of,
